main_summary.summary: Roberta "Birdie" Alexander, interviewed by David Cline on June 29, 2016, in San Diego, California, recounts a multi-generational radical family history and her own activist trajectory through the Free Speech Movement, the Black Panther Party, and the UC San Diego Third College/Lumumba-Zapata College experiment. Born December 1946 in Berkeley to Franklin Alexander (a Black union organizer and director of the Civil Rights Congress in Oakland) and a Lithuanian Jewish mother whose family fled the 1905 Russian revolution, Alexander describes her father's youth in Sioux City, Iowa, his initiation into the Industrial Workers of the World as a 12-year-old harvest worker, and his later organizing for the CIO, ILWU, and Communist Party in the Central Valley and Oakland. She traces her own activism from a 1964 arrest at Sproul Hall through her 1967 deportation from Franco's Spain after speaking against the Vietnam War at the Facultad de Filosofía y Letras in Madrid, her 1969 year in the Black Panther Party national office in South Berkeley working with Elbert "Big Man" Howard on the Black Panther newspaper, a Panther-delegated trip to Japan, and her subsequent academic career as a comparative literature PhD, San Diego City College English department chair, and current organizer with Pillars of the Community on the San Diego 33 case.
key_themes: Black Panther Party internal organization and gender dynamics
Free Speech Movement and 1960s campus activism
main_summary.historical_significance:
This interview is a primary source on the everyday operations of the Black Panther Party national office in South Berkeley during 1969, including the production cycle of the Black Panther newspaper with Elbert "Big Man" Howard and Emory Douglas, the internal class divisions between top leadership (Bobby Seale, David Hilliard, Masai Hewitt, Huey P. Newton) and rank-and-file cadre, and persistent sexual harassment that Alexander names by leadership name. It documents a less-known international Panther delegation to Japan to meet with Zengakuren and farmer-led airport resistance, and the deportation of a Black American student from Franco's Spain in 1967 at the behest of the U.S. State Department.

The interview also preserves the institutional history of UC San Diego's Third College (now Thurgood Marshall College), originally proposed as Lumumba-Zapata College by Angela Davis and Black and Chicano students, where Alexander served as the inaugural resident dean at age 23. Alexander's account links the Civil Rights Congress organizing of the late 1940s and 1950s (her father Franklin Alexander's directorship in Oakland; the campaign that produced "We Charge Genocide") to current carceral-state organizing in San Diego under California Penal Code 182.5, providing rare longitudinal continuity between Old Left, New Left, and contemporary movement work.

chapters.title: Grandfather Between Reservation and Packing House
chapters.summary: Alexander introduces her grandfather, born 1842 and died 1942, who spoke Winnebago, Omaha, and Sioux, worked briefly for the Pony Express, and lived between the Omaha Reservation and Sioux City, Iowa. She describes her grandmother, a Scots-Irish woman who lived mostly on the reservation, and her father's birth in 1914 into a family of ten children where three sisters died young of flu, tuberculosis, and a household fire.

chapters.title: Father's IWW Initiation
chapters.summary: Alexander's father, riding the rails to Minnesota at age 12 to work the harvest, was offered 50 cents a day; Scandinavian bunkhouse workers struck on his behalf to force the farmer to pay him a dollar a day. After a month he was given a red Industrial Workers of the World membership card for ten cents a month. Alexander connects this to her father's earlier exposure to multi-ethnic Sioux City packing-house strikes (Mexicans, Syrians, Poles, Russians, Black workers, Native workers) and Ku Klux Klan terror against strikers.

chapters.title: Mother's Vilna Lineage and the Communist Manifesto
chapters.summary: Alexander describes her maternal grandmother, a Lithuanian Jewish woman from Vilna whose family was anti-Tsarist and immigrated to the United States after the 1905 revolution. Her Russian Jewish grandfather, a St. Louis shoe-repairman, disowned her mother for marrying a Black man, and her grandmother left him rather than disown her daughter. She also recounts her father's encounter as a young shoe-shine boy with a Communist Manifesto pamphlet that led to his joining the Young Communist League in California.

chapters.title: Civil Rights Congress and the McCarthy Era
chapters.summary: Alexander describes her father Franklin Alexander's CIO organizing with cotton compress workers in California's Central Valley, his ILWU work with African American longshoremen, and his directorship of the Civil Rights Congress in Oakland in the late 1940s and early 1950s, where he worked on police brutality and housing rights and was attacked by the Oakland Tribune. She covers her father going underground during the McCarthy period, the family's relocation to her uncle's farm in Perris, California, and FBI vehicles tailing the family on rural roads.

chapters.title: Emmett Till and Los Angeles High
chapters.summary: Alexander recalls her father's first explicit political conversation with her, prompted by the Emmett Till Jet magazine photographs, as her foundational political memory. She describes attending Anytown USA camp through the National Conference of Christians and Jews until her sister was removed as elected mayor after her father's HUAC subpoena appeared in the Los Angeles Times, and the subsequent shooting-into of the family's downtown LA shoe-repair shop across from City Hall. She traces her adolescence at Los Angeles High School and the participation in Woolworth's sit-in demonstrations in Los Angeles.

chapters.title: Berkeley and the Free Speech Movement
chapters.summary: Alexander arrives at UC Berkeley in fall 1964 as a Spanish major and is taken in by Bettina Aptheker and the W.E.B. Du Bois Clubs of America circle through her father's connections to Jessica Mitford and other Bay Area activists. She is arrested in Sproul Hall during the Free Speech Movement at age 17, gives her age as 18 to avoid juvenile court, and speaks at FSM rallies alongside Mario Savio and Aptheker. She also references the earlier Sheraton Palace Hotel sit-in to integrate hotel staff.
keywords: W.E.B. Du Bois Clubs of America

chapters.title: Deportation from Franco's Spain
chapters.summary: Studying abroad in Madrid in 1966-67, Alexander joins Spanish students organizing anti-Vietnam War protest at the Facultad de Filosofía y Letras under director Carlos Blanco Aguinaga. After delivering a speech at a packed building under a "Yankis fuera de Vietnam" banner on April 28, 1967, she is arrested by plainclothes police at her apartment, held at the Puerta del Sol central jail where other prisoners sing "We Shall Overcome," then placed under house arrest. Spanish authorities tell her the U.S. State Department ordered her detention. She is escorted by Guardia Civil to Hendaye on the French border, where Blanco Aguinaga meets her, and CBS flies her to the Hôtel George V in Paris for a televised interview.
related_events: U.S. State Department coordination with Franco regime

chapters.title: Black Panther Party National Office, 1969
chapters.summary: Alexander joins the Black Panther Party in 1969, starting at the breakfast for school children program at an Oakland church where Charles Bursey served meals. Assigned to the Black Panther newspaper at the BPP national office in South Berkeley under editor Elbert "Big Man" Howard, she types articles on an IBM Justowriter and lays out pages at the San Francisco printers with artist Emory Douglas. She describes the BPP leadership tier (Bobby Seale, David Hilliard, Masai Hewitt, Huey P. Newton), persistent sexual harassment by leadership (naming David Hilliard specifically), and her speech on women's roles at the 1969 United Front Against Fascism conference where she first met Angela Davis.
related_events: BPP breakfast for school children program
United Front Against Fascism conference summer 1969

chapters.title: Japan Delegation and Leaving the Panthers
chapters.summary: The BPP sends Alexander and Big Man Howard to Japan on Zengakuren invitations; on arrival they are briefly picked up by the Japanese Red Army before being placed with Ogawa Productions, a leftist film company. Over roughly five weeks they speak at mass demonstrations against the new airport on farmland, meet Korean immigrant workers, and address peasant farmers, financing their return tickets through paid interviews. Alexander returns to find the BPP newspaper photo of the metal-plated, sandbagged South Berkeley headquarters published alongside Berkeley police vamping plans. Citing infiltration, COINTELPRO, her unwillingness to die for the Party, and pregnancy, she walks away; David Hilliard later seizes her library.

chapters.title: Lumumba-Zapata College at UC San Diego
chapters.summary: At age 23 Alexander is hired as the inaugural resident dean of UC San Diego's Third College, originally proposed as Lumumba-Zapata College by Angela Davis and Black and Chicano students. She describes a fishbowl of FBI-style infiltration of both the administration and the student body, including a prison-veteran cadre using intimidation tactics similar to those she had seen in the Panthers, and a gunpoint threat that drove her to Carlos Blanco Aguinaga's house. She quits after nine months and enters the UC San Diego comparative literature PhD program.

chapters.title: City College Teaching and Labor Studies
chapters.summary: Alexander earns her comparative literature PhD with a dissertation on the fictional portrayal of popular movements, declines a sole academic job offer, and teaches adult-school ESL for refugees from Vietnam for twelve years before being recruited to organize the ESL program at San Diego City College, where she works for twenty-five years. As English department chair she diversifies the faculty, and as labor studies program coordinator she introduces occupational safety and health classes and partnerships with the UCLA labor program and Center on Policy Initiatives.

chapters.title: Pillars of the Community and the San Diego 33
chapters.summary: Alexander describes her current organizing with Pillars of the Community, a Muslim-led faith-based organization in Encanto/Southeast San Diego, focused on the San Diego 33 campaign against California Penal Code 182.5 gang-conspiracy prosecutions. She profiles Aaron Harvey and Brandon Duncan, two defendants arrested in 2014 under the statute (Duncan for rap lyrics), and a community writing program that culminated in publication of a nineteen-author collection through City Works Press, with a forward blurb solicited from Elbert "Big Man" Howard. She closes reflecting on her sister's medical career, her brother as a Kaiser chief medical officer, and her regret at never interviewing her mother.

loc_video.caption: Roberta Alexander oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in San Diego, California, 2016 June 29
